title: Feedback Is Everything

content.statement: Systems that don't measure can't improve.
content.question: What are you trying to improve without measuring?
content.equation: Improvement = Feedback Speed × Willingness to Adjust
content.mental_model: A thermostat self-corrects because it measures.
content.business_lens: Analytics → adjustments. No data = steering blind.
content.life_lens: Mirror, scale, mood log = personal feedback.
sop: Pick the one number that best reflects whether a specific system is actually working.
Set up a simple way to track it weekly, even a plain spreadsheet is fine.
Rate your current Feedback Speed and Willingness to Adjust each 1-10 (see calculation, page 6).
Put a recurring 15-minute review on your calendar to actually look at the number.
Change one thing based on what the number says, even if it contradicts your gut.
questions: What are you trying to improve without measuring?
Which number would immediately tell you a system is failing, and are you actually looking at it on a schedule?
The last time feedback told you something uncomfortable, did you adjust, or did you explain it away?

definitions.def: When a system's output is fed back as input, allowing self-correction or self-amplification.

content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10: Feedback Speed (how fast you get real signal) 7, Willingness to Adjust (how fast you actually change based on it) 4. Score = 7 times 4 = 28 out of 100. Higher means fast, honest signal is actually being acted on; a high Feedback Speed paired with a low Willingness to Adjust means you're measuring everything and changing nothing.
content.closing_statement: What you don't measure keeps happening unchanged.

caption:
Principle Nº 104: Feedback Is Everything

Systems that don't measure can't improve.

Ask yourself: What are you trying to improve without measuring?
